response.definition: Scope 1 emissions are direct greenhouse gas emissions from sources that your organization owns or controls. This includes fuel burned in boilers, furnaces, and generators; emissions from company vehicles; process emissions from chemical reactions; and fugitive emissions like refrigerant leaks.
response.whyItMatters: Scope 1 is the most straightforward scope to measure and reduce because you have direct control over these sources.
keyFacts: All organizations must report Scope 1 under the GHG Protocol
Reduction levers: fuel switching (e.g., diesel to electric), energy efficiency, and process optimization
Typical sources: natural gas, diesel, gasoline, propane, and refrigerants
reductionActions: Switch to electric or renewable-powered equipment
Improve building insulation and HVAC efficiency
Upgrade to LED lighting and smart controls
Maintain refrigeration systems to prevent leaks
response.terrnixConnection: Use the Terrnix Carbon Calculator to input your fuel consumption and get an instant Scope 1 breakdown by source.

response.definition: Scope 2 emissions are indirect greenhouse gas emissions from the generation of purchased electricity, steam, heating, or cooling consumed by your organization.
response.whyItMatters: For many office-based and light-manufacturing organizations, Scope 2 can represent 40-60% of total emissions.
keyFacts: GHG Protocol requires dual reporting: location-based (average grid factor) and market-based (contractual instruments like RECs or PPAs)
Grid emission factors vary dramatically by country — from ~0.02 kg CO2/kWh in France to ~0.71 in India
Reducing Scope 2 often has the fastest payback through renewable procurement
reductionActions: Purchase renewable energy through green tariffs or Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs)
Install on-site solar panels or wind turbines
Improve energy efficiency to reduce overall consumption
Purchase high-quality Energy Attribute Certificates (EACs) or RECs
response.terrnixConnection: The Terrnix Calculator includes grid emission factors for 80+ countries. Enter your kWh consumption to see both location-based and market-based Scope 2 totals.

response.definition: Scope 3 emissions are all other indirect emissions that occur in your organization's value chain — both upstream (suppliers) and downstream (customers). The GHG Protocol defines 15 categories covering everything from purchased goods to investments.
response.whyItMatters: Scope 3 typically represents 70-90% of total emissions for manufacturing, retail, and financial services organizations. Addressing Scope 3 is essential for credible net-zero claims.
keyFacts: Category 1 (Purchased Goods & Services) is usually the largest for most organizations
SBTi requires Scope 3 targets if Scope 3 represents more than 40% of total emissions
Calculation methods: spend-based (fastest), activity-based (most accurate), hybrid (recommended)
reductionActions: Engage top suppliers to set their own science-based targets
Redesign products for lower embodied carbon
Optimize logistics and shift to lower-carbon transport modes
Reduce business travel through virtual meetings
response.terrnixConnection: The Terrnix Calculator covers all 15 Scope 3 categories. Input your spend data, transport distances, and waste volumes to get a complete Scope 3 breakdown.

response.definition: The GHG Protocol is the world's most widely used standard for greenhouse gas accounting. Developed by the World Resources Institute (WRI) and the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD), it provides standardized frameworks to measure and manage emissions.
response.whyItMatters: Over 90% of Fortune 500 companies use the GHG Protocol. It is the foundation for SBTi, CDP, and most regulatory reporting requirements.
keyFacts: Corporate Standard: covers Scope 1, 2, and 3 for organizational reporting
Scope 2 Guidance: mandates dual reporting (location-based and market-based)
Scope 3 Technical Guidance: detailed methodology for all 15 categories
Five principles: Relevance, Completeness, Consistency, Transparency, Accuracy
response.terrnixConnection: The Terrnix Calculator follows GHG Protocol methodology and uses IPCC AR6 GWP100 values for all calculations.

response.definition: Global Warming Potential (GWP) is a measure of how much heat a greenhouse gas traps in the atmosphere over a specific time horizon, relative to carbon dioxide (CO2).
response.whyItMatters: GWP allows different greenhouse gases to be compared on a common scale, making it possible to calculate total emissions as CO2 equivalent (CO2e).
keyFacts: GWP100 (100-year horizon) is the standard used by the GHG Protocol and most reporting frameworks
IPCC AR6 GWP100 values: CO2 = 1, CH4 (methane) = 27.9, N2O = 273
GWP20 (20-year horizon) shows short-term impact and is increasingly used for methane-focused strategies
Terrnix uses IPCC AR6 GWP100 values for all calculations
response.terrnixConnection: The Terrnix Calculator automatically applies the correct GWP values from IPCC AR6 for all greenhouse gases you input.

response.definition: An emission factor is a coefficient that converts activity data (like litres of fuel or kWh of electricity) into greenhouse gas emissions, typically expressed in kilograms or tonnes of CO2 equivalent (CO2e).
response.whyItMatters: Choosing the right emission factor is critical for accurate carbon accounting. Factors vary by geography, time period, and data source.
keyFacts: IPCC AR6 GWP100 values: CO2 = 1, CH4 = 27.9, N2O = 273
Grid factors updated annually by IEA (80+ countries)
EPA factors for US-specific sources
DEFRA/BEIS factors for UK-specific sources
response.terrnixConnection: Terrnix maintains a database of 15,000+ emission factors covering 80+ countries, updated annually.

keywords: difference between carbon neutral and net zero

response.definition: Carbon neutral means balancing your emissions with carbon offsets, without necessarily reducing them. Net zero means reducing emissions by 90-95% and only offsetting the small residual amount that cannot be eliminated.

comparison.carbonNeutral: Can maintain current emission levels; offsets balance the total
comparison.netZero: Requires deep decarbonization; offsets only for residual emissions

keyFacts: SBTi Net-Zero Standard requires 90-95% reduction before neutralizing residuals
Carbon neutral claims are increasingly scrutinized by regulators and consumers
Net zero is the gold standard for corporate climate ambition
response.terrnixConnection: Use the Terrnix Calculator to establish your baseline, then model reduction scenarios to plan your net-zero pathway.

response.definition: The Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) is the EU's mandatory sustainability disclosure framework that expands reporting requirements from 11,700 companies under NFRD to over 50,000 companies.
response.whyItMatters: CSRD introduces double materiality assessments, mandatory assurance, and standardized reporting through the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS).
keyFacts: Applies to large EU companies, listed SMEs, and non-EU companies with significant EU operations
Requires double materiality: financial materiality + impact materiality
Phased implementation: 2024 (large public-interest), 2025 (other large), 2026-2028 (listed SMEs)
Standards cover environment (E1-E5), social (S1-S4), and governance (G1-G2)
response.terrnixConnection: Terrnix provides a comprehensive CSRD Omnibus Guide covering applicability, timelines, and ESRS requirements.

response.definition: The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) develops global baseline sustainability disclosure standards under the IFRS Foundation. IFRS S1 covers general sustainability disclosures and IFRS S2 covers climate-specific disclosures.
response.whyItMatters: Over 20 jurisdictions have committed to adopting ISSB standards, creating a global baseline for sustainability reporting.
keyFacts: IFRS S2 is fully aligned with the TCFD recommendations (governance, strategy, risk management, metrics)
Uses single materiality (financial materiality only), unlike CSRD's double materiality
Scope 3 reporting required if material, with safe harbor provisions
Adopted by: UK, Australia, Japan, Nigeria, Singapore, and others
response.terrnixConnection: Terrnix tracks ISSB adoption globally and provides guidance on alignment with existing frameworks.

keywords: task force on climate-related financial disclosures

response.definition: The Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) was established by the Financial Stability Board to develop recommendations for climate-related financial disclosures. Its four-pillar framework covers governance, strategy, risk management, and metrics.
response.whyItMatters: Although the TCFD has been consolidated into the ISSB, its principles remain foundational for climate disclosure worldwide.
keyFacts: Four pillars: Governance, Strategy, Risk Management, Metrics & Targets
Over 4,000 organizations supported TCFD
Now integrated into IFRS S2 (climate disclosures)
response.terrnixConnection: Terrnix provides TCFD-aligned reporting guidance and gap analysis tools.

response.definition: The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) drives ambitious climate action in the private sector by enabling companies to set science-based emissions reduction targets aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C.
response.whyItMatters: Over 7,000 companies have committed to SBTi, making it the leading standard for credible corporate climate targets.
keyFacts: Near-term targets: 5-10 year reduction aligned with 1.5°C
Net-Zero Standard: 90-95% reduction by 2050 with residual neutralization
Scope 3 target required if Scope 3 > 40% of total emissions
Validation process: commit, develop, submit, validate, announce, disclose
response.terrnixConnection: Use the Terrnix Calculator to establish your baseline emissions before developing your SBTi target.

response.definition: Double materiality is the principle that sustainability matters from two perspectives: (1) financial materiality — how sustainability issues affect the company's value, and (2) impact materiality — how the company's activities affect people and the environment.
response.whyItMatters: Double materiality is the cornerstone of CSRD and GRI reporting, requiring organizations to assess both inward and outward impacts.

comparison.singleMateriality: Used by ISSB/TCFD — focuses only on financial risks and opportunities
comparison.doubleMateriality: Used by CSRD/GRI — covers both financial and societal/environmental impacts

response.terrnixConnection: Terrnix provides guidance on conducting double materiality assessments for CSRD compliance.

response.definition: A net-zero strategy is a comprehensive plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 90-95% and neutralize residual emissions, typically aligned with the SBTi Net-Zero Standard.
keySteps: 1. Measure: Establish a complete GHG inventory (Scope 1, 2, 3)
2. Set targets: Define science-based near-term and net-zero targets
3. Reduce: Implement decarbonization levers across operations and value chain
4. Neutralize: Offset residual emissions with high-quality carbon removals
response.terrnixConnection: The Terrnix Calculator helps you measure your baseline. Use the PDF report to communicate your starting point to stakeholders.

response.definition: Decarbonization levers are the specific actions and technologies that organizations can deploy to reduce greenhouse gas emissions across their operations and value chain.
response.terrnixConnection: The Terrnix Calculator identifies your highest-emission categories so you can prioritize the most impactful decarbonization levers.

response.definition: Carbon pricing puts a cost on carbon emissions, creating an economic incentive to reduce them. Mechanisms include carbon taxes (fixed price) and emissions trading systems (cap-and-trade).
keySystems: EU ETS: World's largest carbon market, covering ~40% of EU emissions
CBAM: Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism taxes imports from non-carbon-priced countries
Voluntary Carbon Market: Companies purchase offsets for residual emissions
response.terrnixConnection: Terrnix provides carbon pricing analysis and helps organizations model the financial impact of compliance and voluntary strategies.

response.description: The Terrnix Carbon Footprint Calculator helps you measure Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using 15,000+ emission factors covering 80+ countries.
howToUse: 2. Input activity data by scope (fuel, electricity, transport, waste, etc.)
5. Export data as JSON or CSV
features: Scope 1: Stationary combustion, mobile combustion, fugitive emissions
Scope 3: All 15 categories with spend-based and activity-based methods
Visualization: Charts by scope and category
response.securityNote: All calculations happen client-side. Your data is never sent to our servers.

structure: Page 1: Cover with company name and reporting period
Page 2: Executive Summary with totals and scope split
Page 8: Recommendations based on your profile

fallbacks.unknown: I'm not sure I understand. I can help you with carbon accounting (Scope 1, 2, 3), ESG reporting (CSRD, ISSB), sustainability strategy, or using the Terrnix Calculator. What would you like to know?
fallbacks.offTopic: I'm your sustainability assistant. Ask me about emissions, regulations, the Terrnix Calculator, or our Academy guides!
fallbacks.safety: This information is for educational purposes only. For audit, legal, or compliance decisions, please consult a qualified sustainability professional or your legal advisor.
fallbacks.calculatorNotUsed: I can give you general guidance, but if you use the Terrnix Carbon Calculator first, I can provide more specific insights about your emissions profile.
